ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general croissant sandwich class; no specific ham-and-cheese croissant entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKR covers plain croissant; no filled ham-and-cheese variant code exists. Base pastry code used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for ham and cheese croissant sandwich.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical ham and cheese croissant sandwich weighs approximately 120 g and constitutes a single café-style serving.

nutrient_source.title: Lovers' Ham & Cheese Croissant — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (G00010806)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken directly from the HPB Foodo record G00010806 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for croissant with ham and cheese (FDC ID 174987) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; croissant GI reference (GI ~67, white-flour butter croissant)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a filled ham-and-cheese croissant. GI of 67 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain butter croissant (white flour), which is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. Ham and cheese fillings contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 45.8 × 1.20) / 100 ≈ 36.8 / 100 × 120g serving → GL = 67 × (45.8 × 1.2 / 100) = 67 × 0.5496 ≈ 36.8. Corrected: available carb per serving = 45.8g/100g × 120g = 54.96g; GL = GI × carb_per_serving / 100 = 67 × 54.96 / 100 = 36.8. Note: value in record set to 36.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from cured ham and processed cheese; sodium 300 mg/100g with low potassium offset (118 mg/100g) yields an unfavourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item; if consumed, restrict to a half-portion and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as mustard or mayonnaise, as the combined sodium from cured ham, processed cheese, and the croissant base can substantially exceed the 600 mg per-meal sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (45.8 g/100g) from a high-GI butter croissant (GI ~67) combined with 11.26 g/100g sugar and only 2.6 g/100g dietary fibre drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this item; if consumed, pair with a fibre-rich side salad and choose a smaller portion, as the high glycemic load (GL ~36.8 per 120g serving) will cause a significant postprandial blood glucose spike that is difficult to offset with the modest protein content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~10.5 g/100g from butter croissant, processed cheese, and cured ham exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold; trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from commercial croissant dough; high sodium 300 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this item due to its high saturated fat content (~10.5 g/100g), presence of trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), and elevated sodium (300 mg/100g), all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, arterial stiffness, and blood pressure; if consumed occasionally, limit to a quarter-portion and avoid pairing with other high-fat or high-sodium foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated fat (~10.5 g/100g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), and sodium nitrates from cured ham
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item; the combination of high saturated fat from butter and cheese, trans fat from commercial croissant dough, and sodium nitrates from cured ham creates a strongly pro-inflammatory nutritional profile that may exacerbate joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Ham and Cheese Croissant is a popular café-style sandwich sold widely in Singapore, consisting of a flaky, buttery croissant filled with slices of cured pork ham and processed cheese. The croissant is made from laminated dough enriched with butter, producing a light, layered, golden-brown pastry with a crisp exterior and soft, airy interior. The filling typically includes one or two slices of cured ham — which contributes a savoury, slightly salty flavour — and a slice of processed or semi-hard cheese that melts slightly when the croissant is warmed. Per 100g, this dish provides 406 kcal, 8.2g protein, 21g total fat (estimated saturated fat ~10.5g), 45.8g carbohydrate, 11.26g sugar, 2.6g dietary fibre, and 300mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 67 (medium-high), driven by the refined white-flour croissant base, and the glycemic load per 120g serving is approximately 36.8 — clinically significant for blood glucose management. Nutritionally, this item is energy-dense and high in saturated fat, refined carbohydrates, and sodium, making it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is not halal due to the pork ham content. In Singapore, it is commonly consumed as a breakfast or light lunch item at bakeries, café chains, and food courts. The dish is classified as ultra-processed due to the use of cured meat and processed cheese.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010806 at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest equivalent (croissant with ham and cheese) and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database plain butter croissant entry as analogous food, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.2g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.2g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for croissant with ham and cheese (FDC ID 174987 / closest equivalent) as HPB Foodo G00010806 does not report fatty acid breakdown. These values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singapore café croissant are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the level of this composite dish as of 2026. 3. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on plain butter croissant from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for a filled ham-and-cheese croissant was found in approved sources. 4. Glycemic load per 120g serving = 67 × (45.8 × 1.2) / 100 = 36.8. 5. The input cultural classification of 'Malay' cuisine is corrected to 'Western / Singaporean café' as a butter croissant is a French-origin pastry sold in Singapore café contexts; the dish is not a traditional Malay food. 6. This item contains pork (cured ham) and is therefore not halal. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule does not apply.
